title: Śrī Navakāra Mahāmantra (The Navkar / Pañca-Parameṣṭhi Great Mantra)
summary: The Navkar Mahāmantra is the foremost prayer of the Jains: a bow of respect to five kinds of holy beings rather than to any one named god. In its first five lines the reciter says 'I bow to' the Arihants (enlightened, still-living teachers), the Siddhas (fully liberated souls), the Ācāryas (heads of the monastic order), the Upādhyāyas (teaching-monks), and all Sādhus (monks) in the world. The last four lines explain why: these five salutations destroy all sin and are the very first and best of all auspicious things. It is recited at the very start of worship and of every pratikramaṇa (the ritual of reflection and confession), and is the mantra a Jain returns to throughout the day.
ritualPlacement: In this Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) book the Navkar opens the Daivasika (Devasi) pratikramaṇa as the maṅgalācaraṇa (auspicious opening), immediately before Panchindiya (guru-sthāpanā). It is the universal opening invocation and is used identically across all five pratikramaṇas — devasi, rāi, pakṣik, cāumāsī and saṁvatsarī. As an invocation of the five Parameṣṭhins it carries no pakkhī→cāumāsī→saṁvatsarī word-substitution (that substitution applies to the body of the Pākṣika-derived observances, not to this mantra). It also recurs embedded in other acts of worship and, in the Sāṁvatsarī kāyotsarga, is counted as the final unit after 40 Logassa ('40 Logassa + 1 Navkar').
authorAndSource: Anonymous and aṇādi (held by tradition to be without human author / beginningless); it is not ascribed to a single composer. The first five salutation-lines (pañca-namaskāra) are the ancient core; the final four lines (vv.6–9) form the cūlikā ('appended/explanatory' verses) praising the mantra's fruit. The text is in Ardhamāgadhī Prakrit. Verses as printed follow the book's own readings — e.g. the closing line here is 'paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ' (પઢમં હોઇ મંગલં), the Śvetāmbara reading, rather than the variant 'paramaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ' found in some other recensions.
structureArc: Nine verses in two movements. vv.1–5 are the pañca-namaskāra, the five bows, in descending-but-ascending merit order: (1) Arihant, (2) Siddha, (3) Ācārya, (4) Upādhyāya, (5) all Sādhus 'in the world' (loe). Note the deliberate ordering — the Siddha is the higher (fully liberated) state, yet the still-embodied Arihant who teaches is bowed to first. vv.6–9 are the cūlikā, a single four-line sentence explaining the mantra's power: (6) 'this fivefold salutation' / (7) 'is the destroyer of all sin' / (8) 'and of all auspicious things' / (9) 'it is the first/foremost auspicious one.'
recurringImagery: The unifying motif is the pañca-parameṣṭhi — the 'five supreme ones' — as the complete map of the spiritual path made into objects of veneration: two who have arrived (Arihant, Siddha) and three who guide others along the way (Ācārya, Upādhyāya, Sādhu). The recurring grammatical gesture across vv.1–5 is namo + a dative plural ('-āṇaṁ'), 'reverence to the [class of beings]' — homage offered to qualities and states, never to a named individual, which is why no proper name appears. The fifth bow widens the frame with 'loe' ('in the world'), embracing every true monk everywhere. The cūlikā's controlling image is maṅgala (the auspicious): the mantra is named not merely as one blessing among many but as 'paḍhamaṁ' — the first/foremost — auspicious thing, the source from which other blessings follow.
keyThemes: Namaskāra: bowing/homage to spiritual qualities, not to named gods
Universal opening invocation across all five pratikramaṇas
Veneration of states (liberated) and of guides (monastic order) together

glossary.definition: 'Reverence / I bow to'; the act of homage that opens each of the first five lines.
'Worthy one' / 'foe-destroyer'; an enlightened, still-embodied Tīrthaṅkara or kevalin who has conquered the inner enemies (passions) and teaches the path.
A perfected, fully liberated soul, freed from the body and the cycle of rebirth; the highest spiritual state.
Sanskrit ācārya; the head and spiritual preceptor of a monastic community, master of conduct and doctrine.
Sanskrit upādhyāya; the teaching-monk responsible for instructing other ascetics in the scriptures.
Sanskrit sādhu; a Jain monk (and by extension ascetic) following the great vows; 'savva-sāhū' = all such monks.
'In the world / loka'; widens the fifth bow to embrace all true monks everywhere.
'The fivefold salutation'; the collective name for the five bows of vv.1–5, referenced in v.6.
Sanskrit pāpa; sin, demerit, harmful karma. 'Savva-pāva-ppaṇāsaṇa' = destroyer of all sin.
The auspicious / a blessing; the mantra is declared the 'paḍhamaṁ' (first, foremost) of all maṅgalas.
An appended, explanatory addendum; here the four closing lines (vv.6–9) that praise the fruit of the five salutations.
'The five supreme ones': Arihant, Siddha, Ācārya, Upādhyāya and Sādhu, taken together as the objects of this mantra.
An auspicious invocation placed at the outset of a rite or text; the Navkar serves this role opening the Devasi pratikramaṇa.

howToRead: A newcomer can simply read each of the first five lines as 'I bow to…' and the last four as the reason why, letting the rhythm settle the mind before worship. An advanced practitioner may attend to the precise ordering of the five Parameṣṭhins, the dative-plural namaskāra grammar, and the cūlikā's claim of the mantra as 'paḍhamaṁ maṅgalaṁ' — reflecting on homage offered to states and qualities rather than to persons.

sources.title: Namokar Mantra (Navkar Mantra), text, transliteration and meaning
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the five Parameṣṭhins, the cūlikā/last-four-lines structure, and the standard meaning of the salutations.

notes: Verse text and orthography follow this Anchalgachchha/Vidhipakṣa book as the golden source; e.g. the closing reads 'paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ' (પઢમં હોઇ મંગલં), whereas some other recensions read 'paramaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ'. The mantra is traditionally regarded as aṇādi (without human author); the attribution of the final four lines as a distinct cūlikā is the traditional understanding, not a claim of named authorship. The 9-verse numbering reflects this book's line division; the mantra is also commonly counted as 5 padas + a 4-line cūlikā. The reason the Arihant is bowed to before the (higher) Siddha is a well-known doctrinal point — the Arihant is the living teacher who reveals the path — but readings of its rationale vary by ācārya.
